paragraphs: Introduction

This demo script is intended to be used by Cloudflare SEs to highlight competitive differentiation, tailor demo flow to targeted customer personas and ensure messaging is consistent with other customer touchpoints throughout the buyer’s journey. The script itself is broken into vignettes, with details provided on features displayed, key takeaways to be conveyed and target persona.
Background
Value Driver: Best in class security [& performance] managed in a single pane of glass
Sales Play (if applicable):
Products Featured: Payload Inspection, Advanced Rate Limiting, Bot Management, API Shield [API Gateway in the future], Ruleset Engine [Account level WAF]
Target Audience Persona(s): CISO, CSO, Dir of Info Sec, Dir of Eng, Dir of Platform, SRE,

Description: The Purpose of this document is to guide SEs through the Advanced Security features that Cloudflare has to offer so that our customers can push their security infrastructure from their origin to the edge and protect their assets proactively.
Bundles Enablement
Demo vignettes included in this script are:
Payload Inspection
Advanced Rate Limiting
Bot Management
API Shield [API Gateway]
Ruleset Engine [Account Level WAF]
Please determine which demo vignettes are appropriate for your audience as part of demo preparations. Not all demo vignettes have to be shown.
Roles
During this demonstration, the following roles will be introduced: TBD per use-case
Pre-Work
PLEASE REVIEW VIGNETTES CAREFULLY.  All pre-work will be embedded at the vignette level.
Opening
Demonstration
Payload Inspection [Demo Vignette 1]
Opening Statement:

Using Cloudflare’s Firewall rules you can inspect the body of the request/message that’s being sent to the origin server. Customers may use any of the following to talk about this capability: (they are the same thing):
Payload inspection
Firewall rules body inspection
Request body inspection
Request body matching
POST inspection
POST body matching
Background:

Usually when a browser does a request, it’s usually a GET request. But when you’re submitting things like a form or username & password, adding things to your shopping cart there’s data that’s being sent in the manner of a POST/PATCH/PUT request. And these are examples of request methods that have a body in the message.
Trigger: when a customer says I need to create a firewall rule based on the content of the body of a request.

Example Scenario:  I have a hacker that created a bunch of bogus accounts, fortunately for me they’re being lazy and not changing the phone number each time. Here, you can create a FW rule blocking that phone number.
Quick View:
Action:
Block specific phone numbers:
any(http.request.body.form["billing phone"][*] == "6507097780") or any(http.request.body.form["billing_phone"][*] == "5108867854")
This is looking for the request body form and if either number are there, it gets blocked
Only allow certain file types: allow only txt, pdf, and docx, anything outside of these will be blocked.
(http.request.method eq "POST" and http.request.body.raw matches "filename=*" and not http.request.body.raw matches "filename=\".*\.(txt|pdf|docx)\")
Test commands:
curl -s -w “%(http_code)\n” –data-raw ‘filename=*abc.txt*’ https://example.com -o /dev/null   should return 200
curl -s -w “%(http_code)\n” –data-raw ‘filename=*abc.exe*’ https://example.com -o /dev/null   should return 403
Payload Logging

This feature stems from the overall Payload Inspection functionality but this is specifically to understand what in the payload triggered our Managed Rulesets & OWASP. With the custom Firewall rules, customers tell us exactly what they want to catch in the body/payload, with Managed Rulesets and OWASP, a lot of the times customers wonder what and why a request is triggered and this logging feature helps them understand.
Action: Configure payload logging for a managed ruleset in the dashboard: Source
Open Security > WAF > Managed rules.

To configure payload logging for a ruleset you had already deployed in the WAF, select Edit next to the managed ruleset name. To deploy a managed ruleset and configure payload logging, select Configure next to the managed ruleset under Managed Rulesets.
At the bottom of the page, select Configure payload logging.
After reading and understanding the implications of enabling payload logging, select one of the available options:
Generate key pair using your web browser — Generates a key pair (a private and a public key) in your browser and configures payload logging with the generated public key.
Use my own public key — Enter a public key generated by the matched-data-cli command-line tool.
Select Next
If you generated a key pair in the browser, copy the displayed private key and store it safely. You will use this private key later to view the decrypted payload content.
Select Done
If you are deploying the managed ruleset where you configured payload logging, select Deploy. If you configured payload logging for a ruleset you had already deployed, select Save.
Action: View the payload content in the dashboard
Open Security > Events.
Under Activity log, expand the details of an event triggered by a rule whose managed ruleset has payload logging enabled.
Under Payload match, select Decrypt payload log.

Enter your private key in the pop-up window and select Decrypt. If the private key you entered decrypts the encrypted payload successfully, the Payload match card displays the payload content in clear text.
The private key is not sent to a Cloudflare server. The decryption occurs entirely in the browser.
Closing Statement
Advanced Rate Limiting [Demo Vignette 2]
Opening Statement

The major difference between Advanced Rate Limiting and Basic [previous] Rate limiting engine is that the previous RL engine only accounts for IP and IP with NAT on a single path while the Adv RL engine allows you to combine a variety of counters like IP, Header, cookie, Ja3 and more while matching on a diverse set of values similar to our Firewall rules
Quick View:
In addition, you can also create a custom response on blocks to make it a custom JSON, HTML etc, as well as a response code 4xx
[Internal] Important Note: Rate Limiting engine counts per colo and not globally.
For example, if single session-id cookie spreads out the requests geographically and not hit the threshold
But if IP counting, then most likely it will hit the same colo.
Using the Increment counter:
Match any requests on the zone but only count on this api directory:
Action: Source
Navigate to Security > WAF > Rate limiting rules.
Select Create rule.
Enter a descriptive name for the rule in Rule name.

Under If incoming requests match, use the Field drop-down list to choose an HTTP property. For each request, the value of the property you choose for Field is compared to the value you specify for Value using the operator selected in Operator.
Select the rule action from the Choose action drop-down list. For example, selecting Block tells Cloudflare to refuse requests in the conditions you specified when the request limit is reached.
(Optional) If you selected the Block action, you can configure a custom response for requests exceeding the configured rate limit.
Under For, select the mitigation timeout in Duration. This is the time period during which Cloudflare applies the select action once the rate is reached.
Under When rate exceeds, define the maximum number of requests and the time period to consider when determining the rate.

Under With the same value of, add one or more characteristics that will define the request counters for rate limiting purposes. Each value combination will have its own counter to determine the rate. Refer to Determining the rate for more information.

(Optional) Under Counter, enable Use custom counting expression to define an expression that specifies the conditions for incrementing the rate counter. By default, the counting expression is the same as the rule expression. The counting expression can include response fields.
(Optional) Under Cache status, disable Also apply rate limiting to cached assets to consider only the requests that reach the origin when determining the rate.
To save and deploy your rule, select Deploy. If you are not ready to deploy your rule, select Save as Draft.
RL Parameters: Source
IP								ip.src
IP with NAT support						cf.unique_visitor_id
Country							ip.geoip.country
AS Num							ip.geoip.asnum
Host								http.host
Path								http.request.uri.path
Headers (enter header name)					http.request.headers["<header_name>"]
Cookie (enter cookie name)					http.request.cookies["<cookie_name>"]
Query (enter parameter name)				http.request.uri.args["<query_param_name>"]
JA3 Fingerprint							cf.bot_management.ja3_hash
JSON String (enter key)			lookup_json_string(http.request.body.raw, "<key>")
Body								http.request.body.raw
Considerations:
You cannot use both IP with NAT support and IP as characteristics of the same rate limiting rule.
If you use http.request.headers["<header_name>"] in an API request, you must enter the header name in lower case, since Cloudflare normalizes header names at the edge.
If you use Cookie, refer to Recommendations for additional validations you should implement.
You should not use Headers or Cookie as the only characteristic of a rate limiting rule. Refer to Recommendations for details.
For more information on the lookup_json_string function, refer to Functions in the Ruleset Engine documentation.
Best Practices: https://developers.cloudflare.com/waf/rate-limiting-rules/best-practices/#docs-content
Closing Statement
Bot Management [Demo Vignette 3]
Opening Statement

Our Bot M tool is looking for different signals given off by a request that hits our network and we assign it a score between 1-99 with 1 being we are sure that it’s an automated source and 99 being a human using a browser.
Quick View:
Detection & Scoring

Bot Scores of 1, we finger print a request that comes in. We fingerprint on how it does SSL handshake, options it offered up, the headers, the values of the headers, the order of the headers. With these variables, we have a fingerprint and we look at how this fingerprint may have been challenged on our entire 20% of customers. And if this fingerprint has never been able to solve a challenge then we conclude that this is coming from an automated source. And so if this fingerprint matches those heuristics then they get a bot score of 1.
Other ways of scoring 1:
Behavioral analysis: looking at patterns of how many requests hit a certain endpoint and if we see, if that’s getting outside the norm, they also get a score of 1

Javascript detections: we inject some Javascript into the HTML, within the header section, it’s going to load asynchronously, so it shouldn’t slow the browser at all. The JS will ask the browser: screen size, graphic capability, all of these things that a headless browser wouldn’t have.

Bot scores of 2-99: we still use the same kind of fingerprinting but it also uses Fuzzy Logic of Machine Learning [ML] to give it an accurate score. Currently we are on MLv6 which is the best model to date. Scoring distribution on this model is typically heavy on either ends. Lots of 1-3 and then 70+. This ML version is also very good with mobile applications.
BotM is good at verifying automated vs non-automated traffic but not necessarily the maliciousness of a request. Is it coming from a browser/app or not?
Other scoring sources:

The Anomaly Detection (AD) engine is an optional detection engine that uses a form of unsupervised learning. Cloudflare records a baseline of your domain’s traffic and uses the baseline to intelligently detect outlier requests. This approach is user agent-agnostic and can be turned on or off by your account team.
Note: Cloudflare does not recommend AD for domains that use SSL for SaaS or expect large amounts of API traffic. The AD engine immediately gives automated requests a score of one.

The JavaScript Detections (JSD) engine identifies headless browsers and other malicious fingerprints. This engine performs a lightweight, invisible JavaScript injection on the client side of any request while honoring our strict privacy standards. We do not collect any personally identifiable information during the process. The JSD engine either blocks, challenges, or passes requests to other engines.
JSD is enabled by default but completely optional. To adjust your settings, open the Bot Management Configuration page from Security > Bots.
Not computed: A bot score of 0 means Bot Management did not run on the request. Cloudflare does not run Bot Management on internal service requests that Bot Management has no interest in blocking.
Workers subrequests from one Cloudflare zone to another Cloudflare zone do compute a bot score as it does not consider a site hosted on Cloudflare as internal.
__cf_bm cookie: Cloudflare uses the __cf_bm cookie to smooth out the Bot Score and reduce false positives for actual user sessions.
The Bot Management cookie measures a single user’s request pattern and applies it to the machine learning data to generate a reliable Bot Score for all of that user’s requests.
Bot Score vs Threat Score: Bot Score is different from Threat Score. Bot Score identifies bots and Threat Score measures IP reputation across our services.
Verified Bots: we have a list of pre-verified “good bots”
https://developers.cloudflare.com/bots/reference/verified-bots-policy/
https://radar.cloudflare.com/traffic/verified-bots
Known & verified bots are the same
Google, bing, pingdom,
Action:
Bot Score = 1 Block
But Always start with logging if we can, Managed Challenge is the next recommendation so that we can check out the Challenge Success Rate and adjust
Typically under 3% CSR is good. If higher, the rule may need adjusting since we may be challenging legitimate users
Bot score 2-9:
Serves Static content: static JPEG, JS, and default cached files

You can have this off because if you are challenging, it doesn’t make sense to challenge JPEG, and other cached assets so they can render the challenge. And it won’t hurt origin to serve the cached assets anyways
Managed Challenge: Depending on the characteristics of a request, Cloudflare will choose an appropriate type of challenge, which may include but is not limited to:
A non-interactive challenge page (similar to the current JS Challenge).
An invisible proof of work challenge to the browser.
A custom interactive challenge (such as click a button).
A CAPTCHA challenge.
Bot Score 10-30:

In the latest version of ML, MLv6, it’s rare to see scores in between 10-30 because of the accuracy, but still there may be a few. So, JS challenging this traffic is good because it’s non-interactive.
Testing:
Create a Bot management rule to challenge or block scores of 1
Simply create a command like below and replace it with your domain:
curl -svo /dev/null https://www.example.com
You should see a 403
BotM vs Super Bot Fight Mode: What’s the difference?
https://wiki.cfops.it/display/~bsolomon/Bot+Products+by+Tier
Customer question: “I have Bot Management today, can’t I downgrade to Business and get the same thing?”
99% of the time, no. Most of our BotM customers have a much more complex need than what Super bot Fight mode offers
If the customer downgrades, they lose ALL of the enterprise-level capabilities below. In 99% of cases, that’s a nonstarter.
If the customer applies Bot Management to a specific:
Path? -> can’t downgrade
User agent? -> can’t downgrade
ASN, country, or other field? -> can’t downgrade
If the customer uses:
Behavioral Analysis? -> can’t downgrade
Logs? Workers? Bot scores besides 1 or 30? -> can’t downgrade
Only available with BotM and not Bot fight:
API Abuse Detection: Bot Management for customers with mobile apps & API traffic
Anomaly Detection:
Support: false positive & negative analysis will not be provided to Bot Fight Mode customers
Bot Fight Mode: simple, templated responses + recommendation to turn off if something doesn't work
Bot Management: more detailed, thorough review process + escalations for false positive & negative analysis
Path-level control
Granular bot scores
The ability to mix & match fields like ASN, user agent, or country with Bot Management
Closing Statement
API Shield [API Gateway in the future] [Demo Vignette 4]
Opening Statement
Endpoint management system: Monitor the health of your API endpoints by saving, updating, and monitoring performance metrics using API Shield’s Endpoint Management.
Add endpoints allows customers to save endpoints directly from API Discovery or manually by method, path, and host.
This will add the specified endpoints to your list of managed endpoints. You can view your list of saved endpoints in the Endpoint Management page.
Cloudflare will start collecting performance data on your endpoint when you save an endpoint. For each saved endpoint, customers can view:
Request count: The total number of requests to the endpoint over time.
Rate limiting recommendation: per 10 minutes. This is guided by the request count.

Latency: The average origin response time in milliseconds (ms). This metric shows how long it takes from the moment a visitor makes a request to the moment the visitor gets a response back from the origin.
Error rate vs. overall traffic: grouped by 4xx, 5xx, and their sum.
Response size: The average size of the response (in bytes) returned to the request.
Quick View:
API Discovery: You can discover via Session Identifers or using our ML

You tell us how you identify your traffic today whether it’s via a header [auth or x-auth] or a cookie [session-id]. It takes about 24 hours for the traffic to populate [sometimes faster, depends on the amount of traffic]
Note: need at least 50 unique sessions per endpoint in order to discover
Unauthenticated discovery is going to be released soon but currently this feature requires a session identifier.
Volumetric Abuse Detection [Endpoint management system + Advance Rate Limiting]
Based on your 99th, 90th, and 50th percentiles of your traffic. Cloudflare recommends a rate limiting threshold of what abuse would look like per 10 mins.

Simply, click on the Rate limit recommendation in the endpoint management system and this will take you to our Adv RL engine with the endpoints and threshold already input for you. Simply decide the action and timeframe and set the rule.
Note: It doesn’t make sense to Challenge since it’s API traffic and won’t be able to render the challenges.
Sequential Abuse Detection aka Sequences:
Note: Analytics only for now, blocking will come later
Background:

What is a sequence? It is simply a time-ordered list of HTTP API requests made by a specific visitor as they browse a website, use a mobile app, or interact with a B2B partner via API. For example, a portion of a sequence made during a bank funds transfer could look like:

If the above API received requests for POST /api/v1/transferFunds without any of the prior requests, it would seem suspicious. Think about it: how would the API client know what the relevant account IDs are without listing them for the user? How would the API client know how much money is available to transfer? While this example may be obvious, the sheer number of API requests to any given production API can make it hard for human analysts to spot suspicious usage.

API Shield uses your configured session identifier to build a set of ordered API operations (HTTP host, method, and path) requested per session. We may surface sequences in various lengths depending how API Shield scores the sequences.

Correlation Score: API Gateway scores sequences by a metric we call Correlation Score. Sequence Analytics displays the top 20 sequences by highest correlation score, and we refer to these as your most important sequences. High-importance sequences contain API requests which are likely to occur together in order.
High (96%-100%): The endpoints are unique to this sequence compared to other sequences. Recommendation: Secure this sequence if it is expected or apply limitations if unexpected.

High correlation score sequences may consist of rarely used endpoints (potentially anomalous user behavior) as well as commonly used endpoints (likely benign user behavior). Since the endpoints found in these sequences commonly occur together, they represent true usage patterns of your API.
Low (51%-90%) Correlation Score: The endpoints are sometimes unique to this sequence, but are commonly in other sequences.
Very Low (0%-50%) Correlation Score: The endpoints in this sequence are likely not unique to it and will frequently occur in other sequences.
Limitations:

currently requires a session identifier in order to build and track sequences made by an API consumer. Ensure that you have set up your session identifier(s) in the Cloudflare dashboard by going to API Shield > Settings.
Sequences are currently limited to nine operations in length.
GraphQL Protection:
inspecting the request body instead of the path

We support GraphQL in API Shield for API Discovery and Advanced Rate Limiting. Because GraphQL works differently than a RESTful API, customers will see only one main endpoint in Discovery for each GraphQL method+path+host. Additionally, we do not have schema validation for GraphQL endpoints yet. There are other ways to protect GraphQL APIs, such as malicious query inspection. We plan to release protection against malicious GraphQL queries in on a beta by 04/19/23
Schema Validation:
Background: An API schema defines which API requests are valid based on several request properties like target endpoint and HTTP method.

Schema Validation allows you to check if incoming traffic complies with a previously supplied API schema. When you provide an API schema, API Shield creates rules for incoming traffic from the schema definitions. These rules define which traffic is allowed and which traffic gets logged or blocked.
API Shield supports API schemas using OpenAPI Specification v3.
The accepted file formats are YAML (.yml or .yaml file extension) and JSON (.json file extension).
Limitations: Currently, API Shield cannot validate some features of API schemas, including the following: all responses, external references, non-basic path templating, or unique items.

Mutual TLS [mTLS]: uses client certificates to ensure traffic between client and server is bidirectionally secure and trusted. mTLS also allows requests that do not authenticate via an identity provider, such as Internet-of-things (IoT) devices or mobile applications, to demonstrate they can reach a given resource.
Pre-reqs:
Create a client certificate.
Note: You can only use mTLS with a certificate authority (CA) that is fully managed by Cloudflare. Cloudflare generates a unique CA for each zone.
If you need to use certificates issued by another CA, use Cloudflare Access to upload your own CA.
Configure your mobile app or IoT device to use your Cloudflare-issued client certificate.
Enable mTLS for a host in your zone.
Create an mTLS rule in FW Rules:
Go to Security > Firewall rules.
Click Create a mTLS rule.
Enter the following information:
Rule name: A descriptive identifier for your mTLS rule.
Hostname: The mTLS-enabled hostnames to protect, only showing hosts in your application with mTLS enabled.
By default, your rule will have a configuration similar to the following:
Once you have deployed your mTLS rule, any requests without a Cloudflare-issued client certificate will be blocked.
Check for revoked certs: need to use Regex: https://developers.cloudflare.com/api-shield/security/mtls/configure/#check-for-revoked-certificates
API Gateway: combines our security features with
Management and monitoring: Streamline API management with Endpoint Management and tools like analytics, routing, and authentication.
Logging, quota management, and more: All of Cloudflare’s established features, like caching, load balancing, and log integrations work natively with API Gateway.
Closing Statement
Ruleset Engine [Account & Zone Level] [Demo Vignette 5]
Opening Statement
For any additional custom fields that aren’t available out of the box via UI, you need to use Regex and refer to these fields: https://developers.cloudflare.com/ruleset-engine/rules-language/fields/
Account level WAF:
Account level -> Application Security -> WAF
Account level WAF allows you to create Managed Rules, Custom FW rules to apply to every requests/zone in the account. [Currently, this is only available for ENT Zones but may change]
Deployment:
Deploy -> Deploy managed ruleset or OWASP or Exposed Credential check
Pick Execution scope -> Edit scope -> All incoming requests or Custom filter expression per zone/path/ and additional criteria such as Bot Score for example.
Ruleset configuration same as zone level WAF. You can also set up payload logging in the same manner
FAQs:
Do these managed rulesets apply to all zones by default under an account?

No, think of Rulesets like Lists. Lists will only get active if you reference it in a Firewall rule on a particular zone. Same concept applies to Rulesets, you will need to apply managed rulesets to a particular zone.
Why do I need to make a PUT request instead of PATCH when updating a Ruleset or a rule with a Ruleset?

Currently, we don't support PATCH requests on the /Rulesets API endpoint. It's on our road map. Instead, customers have to use PUT requests instead. One caveat thought: "PUT request is a complete resource replacement. In other words, the changes you make are not merged into the existing ruleset, rather they overwrite it entirely. This requires extra care to ensure multiple users are not changing the same ruleset simultaneously, since they could accidentally revert each other's changes. It is the user's responsibility to retrieve an up-to-date version of the ruleset before making changes and re-submitting it."
If I have WAF zone-level and Rulesets account-level both enabled, which one run first?
Account level Rulesets run first and the WAF zone-level runs second.
Closing Statement
Reviewed By:

paragraphs: Core CF Script
Discovery Questions
Cloudflare Main Pitch
Essentially a giant reverse proxy that sits in between your end-users and your origin server
Leveraging our anycast network, our global network allows your end user's proxied requests to be routed to the nearest Cloudflare point of presence.
Over 330+ cities globally https://www.cloudflare.com/network/
Cloudflare is provisioned via DNS:
When a user makes a request, that request would then be proxied through Cloudflare because the DNS response is a Cloudflare anycast IP address.
We have over 330+ PoPs across the world and we are one of the most interconnected networks in the world.
Our network is within 50ms from 95% (20ms to 80%) of the world population.
The idea is to bring the security & performance as far as possible from your infrastructure and as close as possible to your end-users
What the Life of a Request with Cloudflare typically looks like:
A client makes a request to your website
That request would be picked up by the nearest Cloudflare PoP
At the PoP, we would make different security and performance decisions:
Whether it triggers a WAF rule, Custom Rule, bot detector, or our rate limiter
We can drop/challenge that traffic based on the criteria that you have set up
We determine if the response can be served from our CDN cache
Or if that request can be sent back to your origin for dynamic and uncached requests
Does that kinda make sense on how Cloudflare sits in front of your infrastructure and how a request would be proxied through us?
I know the main focus today is on Security/Performance, so I’ll hop right into that, but I do just quickly want to touch on how we do TLS termination:
We can manage the lifecycle of a certificate at our edge, OR we can use your certs the you generate on your origin
Additionally, you can also generate Cloudflare origin certificates from the dashboard and install them on your origin.
We then perform TLS termination between Cloudflare and your origin server to ensure that the traffic is encrypted from end-to-end.
I just wanted to bring that up as well, since it’s usually a common question around how TLS and encryption works with Cloudflare in the middle.
Cloudflare Applications Services: Core Security Route
Cloudflare Applications Services: Core Application Route
Pre-reqs: Assumption is that you already walked through how to onboard, DNS, SSL. Start after this step.

Quick Actions
Under Attack Mode
Ease of use is key to highlight (just a click of a button to make this security level change)

Highlight our IP reputation database, the immune system of the internet analogy (diversity of traffic on our network and our ability to learn from it and deploy the collective intelligence to our entire network)
DNS

Main Pitch: We are a fully functional DNS provider, in addition to being the fastest and most reliable in the world. And like I mentioned earlier, we can achieve that speed because we provide the same service from every one of our 290+ PoPs.
3rd party site to check speed: https://www.dnsperf.com/
DNS propagation check: https://www.whatsmydns.net/
We are also an API first company so everything you see here on the dashboard can be done via API.
https://api.Cloudflare.com/#dns-records-for-a-zone-properties
Types of records: show we offer all types of records
Upload DNS records [bulk in BIND format]
Full vs. Partial Setup

Full: When customers are using our authoritative DNS we call this a full setup. This means that Cloudflare is handling all of their DNS and there is no other DNS provider in play for this particular site.
Partial/CNAME Setup: We also offer what we call a partial setup where customers can keep their existing DNS provider and forward traffic to us.
Essentially you would import your DNS records here that you’d like to proxy and then create corresponding CNAME records over to Cloudflare within your current authoritative DNS.

Orange vs. Grey Cloud
We also have what we can proxied status or DNS only. We refer to proxied status as orange clouding and DNS only as grey clouding.

Orange (Proxied): Cloudflare is providing all of the services we offer. DNS and HTTP proxy. So we are acting as the reverse proxy for this specific subdomain. Meaning we are obfuscating this IP address from the clients. Presenting our 104 IP addresses instead of exposing the customer’s IP.
When you orange cloud a record it propagates to all of our edge nodes immediately
Grey Cloud (DNS Only): This means that we are forwarding the IP addresses directly. We are not then able to provide DDoS mitigation, WAF, CDN, or the many other services that Cloudflare offers.

CNAME Flattening: Fairly straightforward. Instead of making multiple requests for a chain of CNAMEs, you’d only make one and we’d just return the end of that chain. Offers a slight performance enhancement when you have multiple CNAMEs set up in a chain.
Allows Cloudflare to hide the fact that a CNAME exists so it seems like the domain only has non-CNAME records (e.g., A, AAAA, and MX).

When the CNAME flattening option is enabled for a domain, and a CNAME exists in the DNS response sent by Cloudflare, the Cloudflare server generating the response follows the CNAME chain so that the response contains only non-CNAME data. This effectively creates an A record. When this option is turned off, Cloudflare serves the CNAME record directly to the recursor.

API Shield allows you to secure your APIs using the following security solutions:
Mutual TLS (mTLS) — Blocks traffic from devices that do not have a valid client SSL/TLS certificate with an API Shield rule.

Mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication uses client certificates to ensure that traffic between client and server is bidirectionally secure and trusted. It also allows requests that do not authenticate via an identity provider, such as Internet-of-things (IoT) devices, to demonstrate they can reach a given resource.
Support includes gRPC-based APIs, which use binary formats such as protocol buffers rather than JSON.
Specify the API hosts and Cloudflare will block all requests that do not have a certificate for mutual TLS (mTLS) authentication.
Schema Validation — Protects your origin from invalid API requests or a malicious payload by matching each request with the provided schema.

An API's schema describes an API's expected behavior: the kind of requests it should get and the kind of responses it should provide. Invalid requests that do not conform to this schema can cause an API to behave in unexpected ways, potentially resulting in a data leak. Schema validation identifies invalid requests and responses. By blocking invalid responses, API developers can avoid some types of attacks and help prevent data leaks.
An API Schema defines which API requests are valid based on several request properties like target endpoint and HTTP method.

Schema Validation allows you to check if incoming traffic complies with a previously supplied API Schema. When you provide an API Schema, API Shield creates rules for incoming traffic from the schema definitions. These rules define which traffic is allowed and which traffic gets logged or blocked.
API Shield supports API Schemas using OpenAPI Specification v3. The accepted file formats are YAML (.yml or .yaml file extension) and JSON (.json file extension)
How to configure: https://developers.Cloudflare.com/firewall/cf-dashboard/configure-schema-validation

DDoS Mitigation

If you are using Cloudflare as your Authoritative DNS, then your domain name would resolve to CF’s IP, and if someone send a DDoS attack to those IPs, we would mitigate it. If they use a certain TCP port on layer 4, we would also mitigate it as well as on the HTTP level.
Network Capacity is currently 296+Tbps

The biggest DDoS attack to date took place in the summer of 2021 actually and we were the ones that mitigated it. 17.2 Million request per second,  3 times the size of any other reported HTTP DDoS attack.
This attack was automatically detected and mitigated by our autonomous edge DDoS protection systems. The system is powered by our very own denial of service daemon (DoSd)
https://blog.Cloudflare.com/Cloudflare-thwarts-17-2m-rps-ddos-attack-the-largest-ever-reported/
Always on, unmetered, and done at the edge, closest to your end-user. Every single 232+ PoPs can do this
Traffic is scattered geographically [Anycast]

The rps requirement in a either a per-colo or global threshold that needs to be crossed for traffic matching attack fingerprints. In most cases, these thresholds are 1,000 rps per colo per attack fingerprint
Load Sharing within data centers
Mitigation on layers 3, 4, 7
We mitigate DDoS attacks by identifying DDoS signatures [serving 15% of internet traffic currently so we have extensive visibility and can easily identify DDoS Signatures]

A unique DoSd instance runs on every server in each one of our data centers around the world. Each DoSd instance independently analyzes traffic samples out-of-path. Analyzing traffic out-of-path allows us to scan asynchronously for DDoS attacks without causing latency and impacting performance. DDoS findings are also shared between the various DoSd instances within a data center, as a form of proactive threat intelligence sharing.

Once an attack is detected, our systems generate a mitigation rule with a real-time signature that matches the attack patterns. The rule is propagated to the most optimal location in the tech stack. As an example, a volumetric HTTP DDoS attack may be blocked at L4 inside the Linux iptables firewall instead of at L7 inside the L7 reverse proxy which runs in the userspace. Mitigating lower in the stack, e.g. dropping the packets at L4 instead of responding with a 403 error page in L7, is more cost-efficient. It reduces our edge CPU consumption and intra-data center bandwidth utilization — thus helping us mitigate large attacks at scale without impacting performance.

This autonomous approach, along with our network’s global scale and reliability, allow us to mitigate attacks that reach 68% of our average per-second-rate, and higher, without requiring any manual mitigation by Cloudflare personnel, nor causing any performance degradation.

Host Override changes the Host Header on requests to that URL. https://support.Cloudflare.com/hc/en-us/articles/206652947-Using-Page-Rules-to-Re-Write-Host-Headers

A common use case for this functionality is when your content is hosted on an Amazon S3 bucket. Argo Smart Routing
Argo smart routing - avoids congested paths on the internet to dynamically route traffic via the fastest route.
It is like a Waze of the internet. It just a toggle on our dashboard, once you turn on, On average, We generally see a 35% reduction in latency when we have enabled argo smart routing

How it works: We use round trip time to measure the latency and smart route for TCP traffic instead of using just the typical BGP routing protocol;. It is a good feature when you are expecting Global dynamic traffic.

How we find the smartest route: We do active PoP-to-PoP probes. Our colos/edge servers/pops across the globe actively constantly probe each other so we can compute routes through colos which does not require eyeball traffic for a given origin

We also do active origin probes. Origin probes require an eyeball request to trigger, but the logic is that as soon as one request with Argo Smart Routing enabled is made to a particular origin IP from any colo, then all colos will probe that origin IP for the next 48 hours. That’s why we usually say once Argo smart routing, it needs to take about 48 hours to see the improvement.

Railgun: Locally we’ll run a Daemon in your infrastructure called Cloudflared that connects to your memcache, and we’ll cache the file version that you are sending out. And only send Cloudflare the diff basically between the 2 files. So if you have large html files that are updating frequently, you have the ability to send just a very small change portion of that - helps save some latency as well as bandwidth.

Tiered Caching: uses regional tier 1 Cloudflare data centers to propagate content to Cloudflare’s global network, minimizing static content requests to your server, while reducing bandwidth and total costs.

Tiered Cache Topology: allows you to control how your origin connects to Cloudflare’s data centers to help ensure higher cache hit ratios, fewer origin connections, and a reduction of Internet latency.

Smart Tiered Cache Topology instructs Cloudflare to find the single best upper tier(s) for your origin(s). Cloudflare will identify the Cloudflare data centers closest to your origin and only allow these data centers to pull from your origin, saving you bandwidth and processing power at your origin
Generic Global Tiered Topology instructs Cloudflare to use its representative sample of global data centers as a network of upper tiers.
Custom Tiered Cache Topology allows you to work with Cloudflare’s support team to set a custom topology that fits your specific needs

Argo Tunnel: creates an encrypted tunnel between the nearest Cloudflare data center and an application’s origin server, without opening a public inbound port.  Makes 2 persistent connections from that Daemon to the 2 closest Cloudflare data centers and we’ll use that to keep the connection up at all times so allowing only outgoing traffic.
Waiting Room: our queuing feature for web applications proxied by us. This helps customers' origin from being overwhelmed when experiencing spikes of legitimate traffic.
https://developers.Cloudflare.com/waiting-room/get-started
Requirements:
Cloudflare has to proxy the traffic. Without proxying the traffic, we cannot apply any of the functions.

Clients must enable cookies. The Waiting Room is a cookie-based solution.  Each request that matches the waiting room URL will get a __cfwaitingroom cookie on it to indicate if Cloudflare should queue the request or not.  Currently, the Waiting Room to the origin website in First In First Out (FIFO) order and Random. We will have Lottery orders in the future. The __cfwaitingroom cookie is used only when a visitor requests access to a host and path combination that has an associated Waiting Room. The __cfwaitingroom cookie is encrypted, and cookies copied to another device won't work.
Discovery:
Identify if the application can be proxied? Which Root/ Subdomain with or without path will need the waiting room to gate.
For example:
What part of your application do you want our Waiting room to gate?  → Customer: Registration page

How do your end users get to the registration page, via referrer or by visit directly? → Customer: Two ways: by visiting the covid.example.com directly or redirect from www.example.com to covid.example.com
Do you have a limited time session or register an account? → Customer: There are 10 steps, for each step we allow up to 15 mins
Currently, the Path field cannot contain wildcard like /register/*/login or */register. However, there is an implied “*” at the end.

The Path is case-sensitive today.  Therefore, if you config a waiting room for vaccine.mingsportfolio.com/register/, people can bypass the waiting room if they visit vaccine.mingsportfolio.com/Register/

Disable Session Renewal: If checked means yes, users only have session duration minutes to browse the site. Therefore, if you check this box, the above Session Duration is the total time in the application per visit.

Eg: Session Duration 5 mins. Once a user is out of the Cloudflare Waiting Room and has access to the application, users only have 5 minutes to browse the site. Once 5 mins are up, they will be put back to the waiting room in a new queue. This helps to avoid cases where people submit multiple orders or purchases but affect user experience due to you limiting their activity time in the app, and when the time is up, they will be sent back to the waiting room.

If uncheck means false, a user's session cookie is renewed on every request. As long as the user has activity in the app the cookie will be renewed and they don't need to be put back in the waiting room. The  `Session Duration` is for inactivity time.

Queue All: Cloudflare will queue every single inbound request that comes to Cloudflare that matches the endpoint. No requests will go to the origin. Usually enable this when the origin server has an issue or there will be a burst of request coming in at the certain time, you want Cloudflare to queue all the requests. By showing end users they are in the waiting room so they cannot see where the origin is having an issue at that time. This gives you time to fix issues in the origin server.
When you turn Queue All off, Cloudflare will respect the value that the customer put in the waiting room configuration and let the end-user have access based on the value customer config.
Load balancing

Main Pitch: Cloudflare’s load balancing provides DNS-based load balancing and active health checks against your origin servers and pools. We expand on our existing Anycast DNS network to provide resilience against distributed denial of service (DDoS) attacks by steering away from unhealthy origin servers. Users can also enable Geo Steering, which directs traffic to specific origin pools based on the client’s region.
Load balancing is a service that Cloudflare offers, where we can adjust which origin we are reaching out to. You can also delegate different origin pools, weighted load balancing of traffic.
Load balancing and failover: Deliver traffic evenly across healthy servers, automatically failing over when a server is unhealthy or not responsive.

Active health checks: Set up health checks to monitor your servers at configurable intervals and look for specific status codes, response text, and/or timeouts. Cloudflare monitors your servers from each of our data centers.
Geographic control: Direct visitors in Europe to your European data center, US visitors to your North American data center, or dive deeper and configure traffic at a regional level.

Proximity Steering [GPS Steering]: allows customers to input longitude/latitude coordinates of their own data centers on a given pool to route requests from the Cloudflare PoP or external resolver the eyeball request arrived at to the closest geographical pool within the customers network. Some customers don't only want the path of lowest latency or to route requests based on the region they originated from - sometimes they want to ensure the request goes to the closest PoP possible within their network for eyeball requests, internal services, etc.

One thing to note - when selecting GPS steering, all pools added to the Load Balancer must have GPS coordinates configured. If not, the pool that has no GPS coordinates will be treated as infinitely far away and thus never receive traffic. We provide an alert to our users letting them know that they are missing coordinates on specific pools and to fill them in as to avoid any accidental misconfiguration! If they 'edit' a pool at a later date, we also provide a warning stating that the pool will not receive traffic if they continue.

Session Affinity: When you enable session affinity, your load balancer directs all requests from a particular end user to a specific origin server. This continuity preserves information about the user session — such as items in their shopping cart — that might otherwise be lost if requests were spread out among multiple servers.
Session affinity can also help reduce network requests, leading to savings for customers with usage-based billing.
Note: Cloudflare only supports cookie-based session affinity. Other methods, such as TCP session affinity, are not supported.
The actual process:
Session Affinity automatically directs requests from the same client to the same origin web server
When a client makes its first request, Cloudflare sets a CFLib cookie on the client (to track the associated origin web server).
Subsequent requests by the same client are forwarded to that origin for the duration of the cookie and as long as the origin server remains healthy.
If the cookie expires or the origin server becomes unhealthy, Cloudflare sets a new cookie tracking the new failover origin.
All sessions default to 23 hours unless you set a custom session Time to live (TTL).
The session cookie is secure when Always Use HTTPS is enabled. Additionally, HttpOnly is always enabled for the cookie to prevent cross-site scripting attacks.

Load Balancing Rules: allows customers to create custom logic to be applied on their traffic steering and origin selection decisions. This ensures customers have everything needed for their application reliability solution, without needing to wait for us to build every feature as a first class item. Sometimes a single use case, such as load balancing by path, can make the world of difference for a company's needs. Now we are putting that control in our customers hands.
How: https://developers.Cloudflare.com/load-balancing/understand-basics/load-balancing-rules/create-rules
This example routes certain content requests from our webserver to a different origin pool.

Sharing your Load Balancer with other sites: You can share your load balancer with other sites in your account by creating a CNAME record in the Cloudflare DNS app. This is useful for sharing configurations with multiple other domains, and you do not have to create new load balancers for each site.

You can also configure separate load balancers for each domain and reuse monitors and pools. This is especially useful for changing the failover order for different domains, such as when your example.co.uk server has a different failover priority from example.com or example.com.au.
Best Practices:

Planned maintenance: When making changes to your applications or adding assets, you may want to roll out those changes one origin at a time to ensure they work properly. To avoid an inconsistent user experience when rolling out application changes, you can temporarily configure load balancing not to direct traffic to each origin as you upgrade them.

To temporarily ignore an origin server: Deliberately configure a monitor so that your origin will fail health checks and Cloudflare will consider the origin server unhealthy and route traffic away from it. For example, set the Response Code to an HTTP status code you know your server does not return. Alternatively, you could set a value for Response Body that will not match. For example, you can configure the Response Body in a monitor to look for specific text. Or use the Cloudflare API

Images vs Image resizing: Cloudflare Images is an end-to-end solution that offers storage, resizing, optimization, and delivery. Image Resizing only offers resizing and optimization.

Image Resizing: allows you to resize, adjust quality, and convert images on the fly no matter what device they are using. We cache every derived image at the edge, so you store only the original image.

Resizing fetches the original image and caches it (following the usual rules of HTTP caching, Cache-Control header, etc.). Requests for multiple different image sizes are likely to reuse the cached original image, without causing extra transfers from the origin server.

Resized images follow the same caching rules as the original image and their Cache-Control header is identical. We do not support purging of resized variants. Purging of the original image URL will also purge all of its resized variants.
“Resize images from any origin” will allow Image Resizing to resize images from any URI.
I.e. thantastic.com will serve resized images originating from any URI, whether thantastic.com or elsewhere.
By default, Image Resizing will only resize images which originate from this zone, thantastic.com.
I.e. thantastic.com will serve resized images only if the images originate from thantastic.com.

Polish: reduces image file size by removing metadata (date and time, camera manufacturer and model, etc.), and by compressing images when possible. Smaller file sizes mean faster load times for images and web pages
Auto Minify removes unnecessary characters from your source code (like whitespace, comments, etc.) without changing its functionality.
Minification can compress source file size which reduces the amount of data that needs to be transferred to visitors and thus improves page load times.

Brotli: Cloudflare will select Brotli compression as the preferred content encoding method if multiple compression methods are supported by the client. If the client does not indicate that Brotli compression is supported, then gzip compression will be applied.

Mirage: tailors image loading based on network connection and device type. Devices with small screens receive smaller images, and slower connections receive lower resolution images. This speeds up page rendering so users can begin interacting with your website without waiting for images to download first.
Rocket Loader: improves paint times by asynchronously loading your Javascripts, including third party scripts, so that they do not block rendering the content of your pages.

Railgun [being deprecated and integrated into Tunnel]: to accelerate the delivery of your dynamic content through compression and WAN network optimization of communication between Cloudflare data centers and an origin server, speeding up requests that can’t be served directly from Cloudflare cache.
Compressing content by tracking differences between page versions (down to the byte), only sending the parts that have actually changed.
Maintaining a single persistent connection for multiple simultaneous requests, eliminating network connection latency.
Caching dynamic content that changes frequently or is personalized.
Prefect URLs: allows Cloudflare to ensure that we have cached objects ready for your users when they make a request. Prefetch serves them at once, versus making separate requests for each object.

We do this by prefetching the files listed in a manifest file. The manifest file is returned in an HTTP response header with the rel=”prefetch” attribute. The manifest file tells Cloudflare what to load into our cache to ensure that those files are delivered as quickly as possible. This can be helpful if your page has multiple, large assets that you want to serve from cache.

Secondary DNS

Use-case: Most of our customers come to us to control their DNS in a Full Mode (we are the Auth DNS and control all their DNS entries) OR a Partial Mode (CNAME Setup) wherein they only CNAME specific subdomains over to Cloudflare. Both of these are still sort of a "Primary" DNS Setup in terms of Cloudflare.
When someone says "Secondary" DNS, they only want us to serve their DNS entries in case their "Primary" DNS Provider (also referred to as Primary Master DNS Provider) goes down!!

When someone says "Secondary" DNS, they want us to serve their DNS records to the public, but still maintain control of the DNS entries in some other location. That can be another provider or an on-prem system (Infoblox, Bluecat, BIND server, etc). Deploying DNS in this way is done for a variety of reasons, but the biggest ones are either to mask where the control source lives (not exposing an on-prem DNS server to the public) or to provide redundancy (they don't want to depend on any particular DNS provider) for customers in case one of their DNS providers goes down and they get reliability and 100% uptime for DNS.

Primary-Secondary Setup: Here DNS1 has a list of all the DNS entries and will be the party responsible for updating those entries and sending notifications to DNS2 when those entries get updated or changed. Both DNS1 and DNS2 will have their nameservers listed in the registry as hosting the zone and both will share responsibility for requests for DNS records that come into their edge. If one provider goes down, DNS is naturally self-healing, and the recursive resolvers scan through the list of NS records until they find one from a provider that is responding properly.

Secondary DNS with Cloudflare with Secondary Override Feature: This is a “hidden primary” setup where the customer has control of the DNS entries on-prem but the only visible NS records for the zone are Cloudflare’s. Layered on top of this DNS import technology, we’ve built something called "secondary override" where Cloudflare acts as the secondary DNS, but only Cloudflare Nameservers exist at the registrar and Cloudflare can continue to proxy records as it does now. The goal here is for customers to be able to maintain control of our DNS while still being able to take advantage of our proxy solutions and not have to create multiple CNAME zones. One area of caution here though --this would not actually accomplish redundant/failover DNS in the event Cloudflare DNS fails, it only allows for the management/maintenance of DNS records in another vendor while only Cloudflare Nameservers are in place and used at the registrar.

Multi-CDN or Multi-Managed (Primary Primary Setup): It is possible to use secondary in a way that allows you to run a seamless multi-CDN strategy at the "DNS level" only. Here, you would have a central primary server that a customer controls, with "both CDN providers" (Cloudflare and someone else) importing the DNS entries. This is a hidden primary configuration, so the public will not know the true source of the records. Once the records are imported into both providers (and if the provider supports secondary override the way we do) you can proxy the entries. Once that’s done (and both NS are listed as hosting the zone) requests for DNS will be split between both providers. Requests that go into DNS1 will get their proxy addresses and requests that go into DNS2 will get our proxy addresses. Both CDNs will be able to work in tandem and provide multiple layers of redundancy.

Note: For setup 3. if you also need to proxy traffic in addition to DNS, you have to make sure you keep both services in sync, considering the CDNs and WAFs of these two providers would vary a lot it would be very complicated to manage.
Cloudflare currently supports secondary "in" but not secondary "out", i.e. we can be secondary servers for the primary but we can't be primary for other secondary DNS providers.
Cloudflare can be a secondary DNS provider to the primary as long as the following conditions are met:
Primary can allow access to the zone names from Cloudflare IP space
Primary DNS provider must support Zone Transfer
Include Allow statements
Are not doing DNSSEC - Cloudflare currently discards the DNSSEC records on import
Setups: https://blog.cloudflare.com/orange-clouding-with-secondary-dns/
Secondary DNS as passive backup - The secondary DNS server sits idle until the primary server goes down, at which point a failover can occur and the secondary can start serving records.
Secondary DNS as active backup - The secondary DNS server works alongside the primary server to serve records.
Secondary DNS with a hidden primary [Secondary Override]- The nameserver records at the registrar point towards the secondary servers only, essentially treating them as the primary nameservers.
They need to serve exactly the same records. When we are secondary, records are transferred  over to us via AXFR or IXFR and we store and serve whatever we receive
DNSSEC makes it a bit more complicated but let's not look at that for now
Non-DNSSEC records should be exactly the same and will be as long as all changes are successfully transferred from primary to secondary.
DNS Firewall

Cloudflare acts as a proxy between the client (in DNS this is usually a DNS resolver/Recursive resolver which is responsible for ) and the customer’s nameservers. As Cloudflare proxies DNS answers for those nameservers, it caches those answers and blocks DDoS attack traffic.

In DNS Firewall, Cloudflare sits in front of someone else’s DNS servers and pretends to be authoritative. That is, resolvers ask Cloudflare for DNS answers for zones on the customer’s nameservers. It seems like Cloudflare’s servers are authoritative, but in the background, Cloudflare is actually fetching the answers from the customer’s nameservers. That’s why we call it DNS Firewall. It’s “virtually” authoritative.
What: https://support.Cloudflare.com/hc/en-us/articles/218616807-Understanding-DNS-Firewall
How: https://support.Cloudflare.com/hc/en-us/articles/218616817
DNS queries destined for the provider's nameservers will first be sent to the nearest Cloudflare point-of-presence to the website visitor.
If the proper DNS response is available in Cloudflare's cache, Cloudflare will return the response to the visitor.

If the DNS response is not available in the cache, Cloudflare will query the provider's nameservers in the background to fetch the DNS response and send it back to the visitor. Cloudflare chooses the upstream server probabilistically, most likely to choose the upstream server that is most performant from that metal in recent queries. (If the customer's origin is offline and Cloudflare was not able to reach it, Cloudflare will check the cache to see if there is a stale cached record to answer with.)
Simultaneously, the response from the customer's nameserver will be temporarily cached on Cloudflare to be automatically returned when the next query for that record comes along.
DDoS attacks to the nameservers can be identified and blocked at Cloudflare before those requests ever make it to the provider's nameservers.
This works with DNSSEC signed responses as well. If the customer’s nameservers sign the DNS answer, we will proxy the signed answer just like any other DNS answer.
DNS Firewall looks like this:
Visitor <--> Cloudflare DNS Firewall <--> ns1.virtual-dns-customer.com
In short DNS Firewall proxies customer DNS traffic and caches the results, while also providing the following benefits for customers:
Rate Limit the number of queries per second that hit your DNS servers.
DDoS Protection (we protect customer's upstream DNS from any kind of volumetric attack but we do not offer protection from attacks that use DNS protocol with legitimate requests)
Bandwidth savings (obviously by caching the results)
Faster DNS lookups & etc.
DNS Firewall vs Authoritative DNS
Two key differences between DNS Firewall and Authoritative DNS:
DNS Firewall is for customers who need to speed up and protect full nameservers, while authoritative DNS is for customers who need to speed up and protect individual zones.
In DNS Firewall, the DNS data is not stored on Cloudflare’s nameservers. Instead, it is stored on the customer’s nameservers and DNS Firewall is just a proxy that sits in front of them.

With Spectrum, you can now provide security and performance acceleration for any TCP/UDP based application. You can route MQTT, email, file transfer (FTP), version control, games, anything over TCP or UDP through Cloudflare to mask the origin and protect it from DDoS
We have a lot of gaming customers who have heavy UDP traffic and are also very sensitive to Latency using Spectrum to accelerate and protect their traffic today.
Spectrum is a global TCP and UDP proxy running on Cloudflare's edge nodes. It does not terminate the connection. Instead, it passes through the packets to the backend server.

Configuration Options:

IP Addresses: When a Spectrum application is created, it is assigned a unique IPv4 and IPv6 address. These addresses are not static, they may change over time. The best way to look up what the current addresses are is using DNS. The DNS name of the Spectrum application will always return the IP's currently dedicated to the application.
These addresses are Anycasted from all Cloudflare data centers, with the exception of data centers in China.

SMTP: Spectrum can act as a TCP load balancer in front of an SMTP server. Spectrum will not act as an intermediary mail server. Instead, it will simply pass data through to your origin. The client IP shown on mail will be the Cloudflare edge IP. If the mail server requires knowing the true client IP, it should use Proxy Protocol to get the source IP from Cloudflare. Cloudflare recommends enabling Proxy Protocol on applications configured to proxy SMTP
SMTP servers may perform a series of checks on servers attempting to send messages through it. These checks are intended to filter requests from illegitimate servers. Messages may be rejected if:
A reverse DNS lookup on the IP address of the connecting server returns a negative response.
The reverse DNS lookup produces a different hostname than what was sent in the SMTP HELO/EHLO message.
The reverse DNS lookup produces a different hostname than what is advertised in your SMTP server's banner.
The result of a reverse DNS lookup does not match a corresponding forward DNS lookup.
Spectrum applications do not have reverse DNS entries.

Additionally, SMTP servers may perform a DNS lookup to find the MX records for a domain. Messages from your server may be rejected if an MX record for your domain is associated with a Spectrum application, as the IP address of the server will not match the Spectrum IP address.
Ports: Cloudflare supports all TCP ports
Port Ranges: Spectrum applications can be configured to proxy traffic on ranges of ports. Example: https://developers.Cloudflare.com/spectrum/configuration-options
Edge TLS Termination: When enabled, Cloudflare will encrypt traffic for the application at Edge. Edge TLS Termination toggle applies only to TCP applications.
TLS versions supported by Spectrum: TLS 1.1 & up

TCP based Load Balancing: You can configure Spectrum and Cloudflare's Load Balancing to provide TCP health checks, failover, and traffic steering to bring resiliency to your Spectrum Applications. However, to prevent issues with DNS resolution for a Spectrum application, do not use the same Spectrum hostname as a current Load Balancing hostname
TCP Health Checks: Cloudflare's Load Balancer can be configured to probe any TCP port for an accepted connection. This is in addition to HTTP and HTTPS probing capabilities.

Health Checks are optional within a Load Balancer. However, without one the load balancer will distribute traffic to all the origins in the first pool. With the Health Check enabled, hosts that have gone into an error state will not receive traffic maintaining uptime. This allows you to enable intelligent failover within a pool of hosts or amongst multiple pools

Proxy Protocol: Because Cloudflare intercepts packets before forwarding them to your server, if you were to look up the client IP, you would see Cloudflare's IP rather than the true client IP. Some services you run may require knowledge of the true client IP. In those cases, you can use a proxy protocol for Cloudflare to pass on the client IP to your service.
For TCP, Spectrum supports adding Proxy Protocol v1, which is the human readable version supported by Amazon ELB and NGINX.
When TCP applications are configured to use 'PROXY Protocol v1', Cloudflare will prepend each inbound TCP connection with the PROXY Protocol plain-text header
Format: PROXY_STRING + single space + INET_PROTOCOL + single space + CLIENT_IP + single space + PROXY_IP + single space + CLIENT_PORT + single space + PROXY_PORT + "\r\n"
Example for ipv4: PROXY TCP4 192.0.2.0 192.0.2.255 42300 443\r\n
Example for ipv6: PROXY TCP6 2001:db8:: 2001:db8:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff:ffff 42300 443\r\n
For TCP/UDP applications, use Proxy Protocol v2
When TCP applications are configured to use 'PROXY Protocol v2', Cloudflare will prepend each inbound TCP connection with the PROXY Protocol binary header.
When UDP applications are configured to use 'PROXY Protocol v2', Cloudflare will prepend the first UDP datagram on a stream with a PROXY Protocol binary header.
Cloudflare is also developing a custom proxy protocol called Simple Proxy Protocol which is being released soon. It is a lightweight protocol developed specifically for UDP.

Simple Proxy Protocol dictates that your origin must also prepend packets meant for the client with the same header, including original client source information. This is done to validate that packets coming in are in fact intended for the client.

The API supports specifying an origin by hostname. This is useful for cases where you might setup Spectrum in front of a load balancer like an AWS ELB that offers a hostname instead of a static IP. How: https://developers.Cloudflare.com/spectrum/cname-origins

Logs: Spectrum logs the entire lifecycle of every client that connects through it. These event logs are available through Logpush as a separate category (dataset type spectrum_events); they are not part of HTTP log events.
For each connection, Spectrum logs a connect event and either a disconnect or error event.
Configuration: https://developers.Cloudflare.com/spectrum/logs
Spectrum for FTP: https://developers.Cloudflare.com/spectrum/ftp

For Hostname validation: there are 3 methods:
CNAME:

Full setup: Cloudflare iterates over the CNAME chain starting from the hostname. The value of the last CNAME must contain the domain that owns the custom hostname record. For example, if the domain owning the custom hostname record is saasprovider.com, then the CNAME looks similar to app.example.com CNAME proxy-fallback.saasprovider.com
Partial setup
Add a CNAME to your authoritative DNS to point to the fallback origin: app.example.com CNAME proxy-fallback.saasprovider.com
Add a CNAME to Cloudflare DNS for your domain: proxy-fallback.saasprovider.com CNAME proxy-fallback.saasprovider.com.cdn.Cloudflare.net
To prevent unresolvable CNAME loops, only 10 consecutive CNAMES are followed to find the appropriate Custom Hostname CNAME. The final CNAME must contain <zone_name>.cdn.Cloudflare.net

Apex verification: For verification, the account that owns the custom hostname must also own all A and AAAA records for the apex. To verify ownership, the IP returned for the hostname must reside in the IP prefix allocated to the account.

The few seconds Cloudflare requires to iterate over the CNAME can cause a slight downtime. This is likely acceptable for CNAME verification of Custom Hostnames for staging or development sites. However, Cloudflare recommends verification of Custom Hostnames via TXT record or HTTP token for live production traffic. When TXT or HTTP verification completes and the Custom Hostname shows Active in the Cloudflare SSL/TLS app under the Custom Hostnames tab, inform your customer to CNAME traffic to Cloudflare.
TXT record: Each API call to create a Custom Hostname provides a TXT ownership_verification record for your customer to add to their DNS for ownership validation of the Custom Hostname.

Bot Management [Check the advanced sec script for more details]

Out Bot M tool is looking for different signals given off by a request that hits our network and we assign it a score between 1-99 with 1 being we are sure that it’s a automated bot or script and 99 being a human using a browser
For APIs we are releasing API Abuse detection soon, currently in Beta.
Giving customers the ability to quickly identify their API endpoints using automated API discovery
Offering dynamic rate limiting thresholds per endpoint in 24h from deployment
2 parts:
API Discovery: Automatically list all authenticated API endpoints.
Takes around 1 day to index your traffic.
The idea here is that even if you don’t have a schema, Cloudflare can generate that based on the information that you give us.
Smart Rate Limiting: Automatically suggest rate limits for each endpoint.
We built out this scoring system based on:
Heuristics (Bot Score = 1): Before making any other calculations, the incoming request must pass a number of heuristic checks.
If any of these heuristics are triggered, the Bot Score for that request is immediately set to 1 - the lowest possible score.
Empty User Agent - The device making the request did not specify a User Agent (UA) at all. Any legitimate browser will always send the appropriate UA for that browser.

Bot User Agent - The device making the request explicitly called itself out as a bot. (e.g. Google search crawlers will specify "Googlebot"). In this case there is no need for additional checking, because the bot has already confessed its true nature.

Fingerprint Match - A fingerprint generated from connection signatures (e.g. the TLS ClientHello or the HTTP/2 signature) matches a known fingerprint indicating an automated source. Agents such as curl, python, and csharp have unique fingerprints for which we can definitively say the request is coming from one of those sources.
JavaScript Injection (codename: "Picasso") - Additional heuristic detection and fingerprinting (read more in the Picasso section below)
Machine learning (Bot Score = 2-99): If all heuristics above are passed without triggering, then it is up to our Machine Learning (ML) model to generate a score for the request.
First, a signature is built for that request, comprising the following properties and others:
SSL mode
Cookies included
Rendering mode (quirks)
Accept-Language
Referer presence
Presence, order, and case of various request headers
This signature is then applied using our Machine Learning model to a subset of historical data across our entire customer base and all web properties behind Cloudflare.
The ML model generates a score from 2 to 99 based on this signature when compared to the data.

Behavioral Analysis [Anomaly Detection]: For certain types of traffic the Machine Learning model will continue to generate high scores for requests which look legitimate, but these agents are making requests at unusually high volume - for example, an actual Chrome browser being used to automatically scrape thousands of images per minute.
BAD is effectively a form of adaptive rate limiting, which will mitigate attacks from agents which are good at bypassing the Machine Learning detection.
Today, if a single IP triggers BAD, requests from that IP to that zone will get a score of 1 for the next 24 hours, regardless of other scoring components.
How Behavioral Analysis works:
First, a baseline traffic pattern of "typical" traffic per IPs is established for a specific zone over a 24hr period.
If a threshold is surpassed for a particular IP (e.g. requests per second 10x the baseline) then the Bot Score will be lowered for requests coming from that IP.
Currently, all requests from that IP will have a bot score of 1 for the next 24 hours.
This is useful in cases where a single IP is performing anomalous behavior but the requests on an individual basis look legitimate and human.

BotM Cookie: Once a zone has been entitled for Bot Management, Cloudflare will enable a "Bot Management" session cookie in order to smooth out the Bot Score and reduce false positives for actual user sessions.
The Bot Management cookie will measure a single user's request pattern and apply it to the machine learning data to generate a reliable Bot Score for all of that user's requests.

Based on a 1% sample so need to multiply to match google analytics.

Customer question: “I have Bot Management today, can’t I downgrade to Business and get the same thing?”
