Websockets

WebSockets are open connections sustained between the client and the origin server. Inside a WebSockets connection, the client and the origin can pass data back and forth without having to reestablish sessions. This makes exchanging data within a WebSockets connection fast. WebSockets are often used for real-time applications such as live chat and gaming.
Implementation: No additional configuration is required to send WebSockets traffic through Cloudflare. Cloudflare will immediately begin proxying your WebSockets through to your origin.
If they want They want argo with web sockets and we would need to do spectrum to be able to do this

The initial HTTP 101 request is subject to the WAF, rate limiting, and other firewall features just like any other WebSockets connection. However, once a connection has been established the WAF does not perform any further inspections.
NOTE: When Cloudflare releases new code to its global network, we may restart servers, which terminates WebSockets connections.
Headers: When a browser initiates a web socket request, we keep all the Cloudflare headers intact when we proxy the request back to the origin (e.g. client IP, client Country etc).
Handshake: client -> Cloudflare -> Origin -> Cloudflare -> Client
Stream

Why we built this: Customers were doing lots of integration work – integrating a storage solution like S3 with an encoding solution like Elemental with a CDN like Cloudflare with a player like video.js - just to get video to work. We wanted to streamline this.
Provides end-to-end video infrastructure at scale. Stream handles storage, encoding and adaptive bitrate playback for you so you can focus your core idea.
One thing to note: Your stream videos are not attached to a domain in your Cloudflare account and you don't need a domain on Cloudflare to use Stream.

Videos are delivered to the stream player using the Cloudflare managed videodelivery.net domain. Stream delivery bandwidth is not counted towards customer bandwidth or displayed in zone analytics. Workers, page rules or caching changes can not be placed on top of Stream videos. Caching and delivery is managed by Cloudflare and billed to the customer on a basis of minutes delivered.
Stream takes care of optimizing for every device and browser, allowing for the highest viewing quality possible given the user's Internet speed (adaptive bitrate streaming)
The Cloudflare player supports VAST ads.
Ways to upload:

We support the TUS protocol to do resumable uploads with tus [for larger files], which is used by Vimeo and others. (For more information visit tus.io). TUS supports reliable and resumable uploads. There are libraries and clients that support tus (https://tus.io/implementations.html) and it is not very hard to implement the protocol yourself from the spec (https://tus.io/protocols/resumable-upload.html).
You can upload from your local storage by leveraging our UI

You can also upload via a link. We support Copy from Cloud where the customers can submit publicly accessible URLs to Stream and we will pull it from their cloud storage to Stream. This is an easy solution if the customer already has their videos in a cloud.

But the best and most efficient way to upload would be to use the API. We support upload via a single HTTP Post call: if the video fits in a single HTTP request of size 200MB and below, this is the simplest option and does not require any integrations. This method works in every environment and device easily.
First you can grab your global API key
Then you can just copy the cURL command and input the Video url, video name, & API key
Full API documentation: https://api.Cloudflare.com/#stream-videos-upload-a-video-from-a-url

Webhooks: you can use webhooks to notify your service when videos successfully finish processing and are ready to stream. You will need a Cloudflare API token and then just run cURL. When a video on your account finishes processing, you will receive a POST request notification with information about the video.
Security:

​Signed URLs / Tokens: By default, videos on Cloudflare Stream can be viewed by anyone anytime until you delete the video. Users can view the source of the embed code in their browser and get a URL to the video that could be shared with others. To prevent this, use signed URLs.
To implement signed URLs:
Create a key: Upon creation you will get a RSA private key in PEM and JWK formats. Keys are created, used and deleted independently of videos. Every key can sign any of your videos.
Make a video require signed URLs: click on the UI. Restricting viewing can be done by updating the video's metadata.
Sign tokens to use in embed code: After creating a key, you can use it to sign unique signed tokens. These tokens can be used in place of video ids in the stream embed code.

For security reasons, the key signing a token to view a video must be associated with the same account the video was uploaded to. For example, if you have a key owned by account A attempting to sign a token for a video owned by account B, that token will not be accepted.
Stream Delivery
Chunking/Large File Delivery

One inherent flaw with video delivery over HTTP is file size. Video files are fairly large.  In legacy applications, web servers would typically be required to send the entire file over to the client before the user was able to watch the video. This is not a great experience because if the video is 1GB, it could take quite some time to start playing.

The solution to this problem is "chunking". The idea is that rather than download one large file, the client will download subsequent smaller files as the video progresses, meaning that the user is able to start watching video much earlier. Similarly, there's now more control over what quality of video to send - for example, if you are watching video on your phone and need to switch from your fast wifi to slow mobile network, you can dynamically switch from large file sizes to small file sizes.

When a HLS video is requested, the manifest (.m3u8) is requested, which a very small file that references video assets. The manifest informs the client what video to start watching. This video is usually sent in a transport file: .ts.
Tiered Caching and Egress
As Cloudflare's networks grows larger and larger, customers understandably becoming increasingly concerned about egress "cost", both from a monetary perspective and a performance perspective.
Tiered caching helps alleviate these concerns, but our default topology is not one size fits all.
With our default topology, we do our best to map a good balance of tier 1 PoPs around the world to best suite the need of our customers.

With Custom Tiered Topology: Since we know the location of the origin, we can define a single (with a failover) origin, generally in a close proximity. This will help speed up the delivery of the assets to the origin, and reduce the egress cost from the origin

When someone requests the file from the origin once, and then you stream that file to the first person requesting it. And then everyone else has to wait for that file to be populated. Which in the case of like large software deployments, let’s say windows OS update, you remember how large those updates were. Those things took forever to download or even like Mac OS catalina or something. That would take hours to download. Generally that wouldn’t work and we wouldn’t be able to serve that to everyone. We do have a max file upload limit anyways so this wouldn’t necessarily be the case.

Let’s say the file download takes like 30 secs, now a user who has made the request is stuck waiting for that file to download at our edge and then they can download it and so it takes a long time and you might have connection limits on ur origin or timeouts and the user never really gets the file.

Now what we do is we download the file from the origin once then we stream it to the first person who requested it and we stream it everyone else so you don’t have to wait for it to download, you get it as we are getting it. In general, that works really well for video because the pattern of usage is like a live stream started, ok, click play on the warriors game and then you get the manifest file every 10 seconds and the manifest file shows you other files that you need to download. Now every 10 seconds, you have 1000s of people coming into our edge trying to find an asset that isn’t there and thats where you can easily run into buffering issues where like 10 seconds after you requested something, the file itself is taking 30 seconds to download and now you’ve seen like 10 secs of the game, and then you get the lil beach ball loading sign aka the circle of death where it’s loading for the next 10 seconds and the cycle repeats over & over again.
So instead, we would just stream it as we get it to you. So, in that same use-case you’re getting that file as we’re getting it so you don’t get as much buffering in theory.

What if you had a file you wanted authentication in front of. For example, like a udemy or coursera course where you have to pay for the access to a file that’s already there. Like econ 101 course, if you were to buy that in general you would some sort of password and token in order for you to access the actual video to access that econ 101 course.
In the stream world, we need the ability to differentiate between ppl who are authenticated vs who isn’t. Or you’d be serving this video up for free and wouldn't be making any money

So, we have authentication built into workers and FW as part of our stack that works with the stream product. This allows you to do signed URLs, and other sorts of authentication schemes to protect the file.
Regional Services

Due to the way routing works on the internet, and our anycast network, by default we can't guarantee that traffic will stay in the country, and there were probably routing or load related reasons that it ended up terminating in Frankfurt.

In order to deploy our security services, Cloudflare must terminate TLS in our edge datacenters and inspect HTTP request and response payloads. I'd like to recommend that if their sensitive information is required to stay in a particular country at all times, they consider Regional Services, where they can specify the countries in which their sensitive traffic will be decrypted: https://www.Cloudflare.com/data-localization/
Regional services would guarantee the traffic was never unencrypted outside a region, in this case the US.
Emphasize that in this situation, it's still possible traffic would be routed to frankfurt (or wherever else). but the customer could rest assured that Frankfurt was only acting as a TCP proxy.

With Regional Services enabled, content traffic is ingested on Cloudflare’s global Anycast network at the location closest to the client, where we can provide L3 and L4 DDoS protection. Instead of being inspected at that data center, this traffic is securely transmitted to Cloudflare data centers inside the region selected by the customer.
tables: Cloudflare ensures your resources connected to the internet are secure, performant, and reliable. When loading a zone into Cloudflare you are provided with options for organization to have several websites/zones. Each zone can be edited with their individual unique policies. Everything in the dashboard or API is logged here (can denote which by API label).  Audit record tied to actions each user takes within the dashboard and this is tied to APIs as well Audit logs are for each organization.

We are a fully functional DNS provider, in addition to being the fastest and most reliable in the world. And like I mentioned earlier, we can achieve that speed because we provide the same service from every one of our 330+ PoPs. Proxied status in Cloudflare tells what subdomains and records will pass through modules at our edge. We refer to proxied status as orange clouding and DNS only as gray clouding. When you orange cloud a record, it propagates to all of our edge nodes immediately, while gray clouding allows for compatibility of records like MX (passing traffic directly to the origin ip).

Through Universal SSL, Cloudflare is the first Internet performance and security company to offer free SSL/TLS protection. Cloudflare SSL/TLS also provides a number of other features to meet your encryption requirements and certificate management needs from flexible (for debugging and development) all the way over into full strict configurations (encrypting everything end to end).

Cloudflare issues, renews free, unshared, publicly trusted SSL certificates to all Cloudflare domains. Additionally, there are many flexible and togglable configurations that allow users to choose between certificate authorities, TLS versions, and other settings that will directly affect how users on the internet will reach the cloudflare domain.

Origin Certificate Authority encrypts traffic between cloudflare points of presence to your origin web server (all with the benefit of reducing bandwidth consumption). This module is most relevant when you choose the Full (strict) configuration.

This demo script is intended to be used by Cloudflare SEs to highlight competitive differentiation, tailor demo flow to targeted customer personas and ensure messaging is consistent with other customer touchpoints throughout the buyer’s journey. The script itself is broken into vignettes, with details provided on features displayed, key takeaways to be conveyed and target persona.
Background
Value Driver: Zero Trust Network access & Secure Web Gateway managed in a single pane of glass with CF core services
Sales Play (if appliable): https://cloudflare.showpad.biz/webapp2/results?query=zero%20trust&scope=content&slug=97b8cdfe-7d01-47fb-9768-4676d2fe3b79
Products Featured: WARP, Gateway, Access, RBI, DLP profiles, CASB, Tunnels
Target Audience Persona(s): CISO, CSO, Dir of Info Sec, Head of IT, IT Manager

Description: The Purpose of this document is to guide SEs through the Zero-Trust features that Cloudflare has to offer so that our customers can protect inbound traffic to their applications/networks as well as outbound traffic from users & devices in a Zero-Trust model.
Demo vignettes included in this script are:
Overview, Settings, WARP
Access & Tunnels
Gateway, RBI, DLP
CASB
Please determine which demo vignettes are appropriate for your audience as part of demo preparations. Not all demo vignettes have to be shown.
Roles
During this demonstration, the following roles will be introduced:
Pre-Work
PLEASE REVIEW VIGNETTES CAREFULLY.  All pre-work will be embedded at the vignette level.
Opening
Demonstration
Overview, Settings, WARP [Demo Vignette 1]
Opening Statement:

Cloudflare Zero-trust runs on our Anycast network and in the same manner as our Core services, our ZT services run on every single PoP. So instead of a traditional VPN where you have a single onramp to a network, Devices will be able to onramp from a CF PoP closest to the end user so essentially over 285+ on-ramp locations for the devices. In this closest CF PoP is where all of the Gateway, device posture checks and policies are vetted before they get let through to the public internet or to an application/network behind Access
I find that a request flow diagram works very well in showcasing how the traffic flows from end-to-end. Feel free to make a copy of this slide or create your own: Than's Cloudflare One
In this section, we will go over how to set up your Cloudflare ZT dashboard to configure your idP, device enrollment permissions, Deployment methods, Device trust, split-tunneling, WARP profiles.
Home:
ZT Life of a Request: Source
Zero Trust: Core Route
Zero Trust: Access Route
Zero Trust: Gateway Route
Deepdive into each product below
Settings:
General Settings:
Team Domain: Important to pick a team domain name that fits your organization.
domains are formatted as: teamname.cloudflareaccess.com
This is where the App Launcher lives, where the idP makes callbacks, and where users make access requests to applications behind Access.
Customization: Block page & Login Page. Here you can customize your Login and Block pages to tailor this to your organization’s needs and policies.
API/Terraform read-only mode: Lock all settings as Read-Only in the Dashboard, regardless of user permission. Updates may only be made via the API or Terraform for this account when enabled.
Authentication:
By default, One-Time Pin [OTP] is already added as an authentication measure but here’s where you can add all of the idPs your organization work with.

You can select from the pre-populated list of providers or add any vendor using the OIDC or SAML option. Follow the appropriate steps outlines in the dashboard or from your preferred vendor by creating an application for Cloudflare in your idP dashboard.
SCIM is available for Okta & Azure AD today.
Note, you cannot use the same idP as authentication and also put it behind Access. Ex: G-Suite as idP and Google Workspace as an Access application or Azure AD as idP and m365 as Access Application.
WARP Client:
Device Enrollment Permissions: Define who can connect devices to your organization/log into your org using WARP.
Session Duration: Set a session duration for device enrollment permissions. Once a session expires, users will be asked to log back in
Define granular device enrollment rules/permissions.
Device Settings/WARP profiles: Set default and customized configurations for groups of devices in your organization
example: Default employees vs Contractor
Determine settings like Captive portal detection, lock WARP switch, auto connect, etc.

Local Domain Fallback: Configure Cloudflare Zero Trust to ignore DNS requests to a given list of domains. These DNS requests will be passed back to other DNS servers configured on existing network interfaces on the device.
By default Cloudflare ignores local DNS like .corp, .internal, etc. Delete an entry if you want to route that traffic through WARP

You can add additional domains to the Local Domain Fallback list and specify a DNS server to use in place of the Gateway resolver. The WARP client proxies these requests directly to the configured fallback servers.
Add a domain:
Navigate to the Local Domain Fallback page.
Enter the Domain you want to exclude from Gateway. All prefixes under the domain are subject to the local domain fallback rule (for example, all entries are interpreted as \*.example.com).

Enter the DNS server(s) that should resolve that domain name. All servers are tried and the fastest response is used. It is best to always specify at least one DNS server that Local Domain Fallback should use for any domain you add. If a value is not specified, the WARP client will try to identify the DNS server (or servers) used on the device before it started, and use that server for each domain in the Local Domain Fallback list.
Enter an optional description and select Save domain.
The domain will appear in the list of Local Domain entries.
Delete a Domain: click on the record and delete

Spilt Tunnels:Configure Cloudflare Zero Trust to exclude or include traffic to a given set of IP addresses or domains. Any traffic directed to an excluded destination will be handled by the local machine. Use wildcards to match against multiple subdomains at the same time.
Include IPs and Domains: enter only IPs or Domains that you want to route through WARP. Warp will ignore all other traffic in this mode.

Exclude IPs and Domains [More popular & preferred]: enter IPs or Domains that you want to not route through WARP. For example, there are several default entries in here like 10. or 192. if you want to route traffic to private networks [Warp to Tunnel], delete the appropriate IPs from the list.
Global Settings:
Admin Override: Only allow users to disable the WARP client with a one-time use password.
Device Posture:
WARP Client Checks: checks will be performed on your devices by the WARP client. WARP can check for existence of any application, file, serial number, etc.
Device Posture providers: 3rd party initiated posture checks, essentially this prompts an API call to the 3rd party provider before the request gets allowed/blocked.
Vendors: Workspace ONE, Crowdstrike, Uptycs, Microsoft Endpoint Manager, Tanium. More on the way

Service Provider Checks: These checks will be performed on your devices by third-party service providers you have integrated with the WARP client. This is where you will define polices from the 3rd party.
ex:

Managed Networks: allows you to selectively apply WARP client settings if the device is connected to a secure network location such as an office.
https://developers.cloudflare.com/cloudflare-one/connections/connect-devices/warp/configure-warp/managed-networks/
Network:
Gateway Logging:
Activity Logging: decide how you want to capture Gateway logs, from all to blocked only to nothing.
Exclude PII: Decide if you want to exclude PII, if you enable this feature, the policies that get triggered will not have a User tied to it.

Enhanced File Detection: is an optional feature to extract more file information from HTTP traffic. When enabled, Gateway will read file information from the HTTP body rather than the HTTP headers, offering greater accuracy and reliability. This feature may have a minor impact on performance for file-heavy organizations.
To enable:
Go to Settings > Network.
Enable TLS decryption.
Turn on Enable enhanced file detection.

DLP Payload Encryption public key: Input your public key to access payload log match data. Your key will be used to encrypt all payload logs recorded via DLP payload logging.
Firewall:
Proxy: Forward traffic to Gateway to filter both outbound traffic as well as traffic directed to resources connected via a Cloudflare Tunnel, GRE tunnel, and/or IPsec tunnel. TCP, UDP, and/or ICMP
TLS Decryption: Inspect encrypted HTTP traffic from enrolled devices.
need this on for HTTP policies, RBI to function
You can enable only cipher suites and TLS versions compliant with FIPS 140-2.
AV Inspection: Define your anti-virus scanning preferences on the Layer 7 firewall

Note: CF cannot scan anything larger than 15MB, so uncheck this option if you will have users downloading files larger than the limit. Otherwise, leaving this option on will block anything larger than 15MB.
Split tunnels and local domain fallback: now managed under Warp -> Profile -> Configure. More details in the Warp Client section.

WARP deployment:
First Time Deployment:
Create a Cloudflare Zero Trust account & team name
Set up a login method by using OTP and/or set up 3rd party idP
Define device enrollment permissions

Install the Cloudflare root certificate on your devices. HTTP traffic inspection require users to install and trust the Cloudflare root certificate on their machine or device. If you are installing certificates manually on all your devices, these steps will need to be performed on each new device that is to be subject to HTTP filtering.
You can include the CF cert in the mobile config during the managed deployment
Download and deploy the WARP client to your devices
Login. Warp -> Gear button/Setting -> Preferences -> Account -> Login to CF Zero trust
Managed Deployment: Deploy using an MDM
ex: Jamf, Intune,
Note: need to deploy via a mobile config and not by a push command in Jamf or Intune.
Access [Demo Vignette 2]
Background:

Traditional VPNs are old, slow, and inherently expose a lot of risk by establishing a direct connection into your network on top of needing constant support, maintenance, and investment to keep it running

The network-level access and default trust granted by VPNs create avoidable security gaps by inviting the possibility of lateral movement within your network. Attackers may enter your network through a less-sensitive entry point after stealing credentials, and then traverse to find more business-critical information to exploit.
Opening Statement
With Access, you can determine different combinations of policies someone must pass before being granted conditional access to a particular application.

In this section, I’m going to diving into Services like Tunnels aka our app connector as well as discussing VPN replacement forcing Zero Trust access assigned to self-hosted apps, private IPs/networks and SaaS applications

Within minutes, you can create a tunnel for your application traffic and route it based on public hostnames or your private network routes. The dashboard will provide a string of commands to copy and paste into your command line to facilitate initial routing configurations. From there, Cloudflare will manage your configuration automatically.

With the IdP connection we set up previously, Cloudflare Access acts as an aggregation layer for your existing security tools. With support for over a dozen identity providers (IdPs) like Okta, Microsoft Azure AD, Ping Identity, or OneLogin, you can link multiple simultaneous IdPs or separate tenants from one IdP. This can be particularly useful for companies undergoing mergers or acquisitions or perhaps going through compliance updates.

In a ZTNA implementation, this linkage lets both tools play to their strengths. The IdP houses user stores and performs the identity authentication check, while Cloudflare Access controls the broader Zero Trust rules that ultimately decide access permissions to a broad range of resources.

Similarly, admins can integrate common endpoint protection providers like Crowdstrike, SentinelOne, Tanium or VMware Carbon Black to incorporate device posture into Zero Trust rulesets. Access decisions can incorporate device posture risk scores for tighter granularity.

Obtain the following URLs from your SaaS application account:

Assertion Consumer Service URL: The service provider’s endpoint for receiving and parsing SAML assertions.
Select your Application from the drop-down menu. If your application is not listed, enter a custom name in the Application field and select the textbox that appears below.
Enter the Entity ID and Assertion Consumer Service URL obtained from your SaaS application account.

The toggle does not impact the ability for users to reach the application. Users with no access to the application will not see it in the App Launcher regardless of whether the toggle is enabled. Users with access to the application will still be able to reach it with a direct link.

If you are having issues specifying a custom logo, check that the image is served from an HTTPS endpoint. For example, http://www.example.com/upload/logo.png will not work. However, https://www.example.com/upload/logo.png will.
Next, choose the Identity providers you want to enable for your application.
Turn on Instant Auth if you are selecting only one login method for your application, and would like your end users to skip the identity provider selection step.
Select Next.
Add an Access Policy
Policies -> Add a policy
Name the policy and and choose the action if the policies match. Choose a Session duration as well ( you can choose “same as application session timeout”
Allow action allows users that meet certain criteria to reach an application behind Access
Block action prevents users from reaching an application behind Access

Bypass action disables any Access enforcement for traffic that meets the defined rule criteria. This may be useful if you want to ensure your employees have direct permanent access to your internal applications, while still ensuring that any external resource is always asked to authenticate
Service Auth rules enforce authentication flows that do not require an identity provider IdP login, such as service tokens and mutual TLS. For example, Include Valid Certificate.

So if we look at my policy above, you can see that I have added 2 login methods and a requirement to be in the United States as well as my ZT instance of Warp client in order to access this application.
Important note: Rule Types
Include: The Include rule is similar to an OR logical operator. In case more than one Include rule is specified, users need to meet only one of the criteria.
Exclude: The Exclude rule works like a NOT logical operator. A user meeting any Exclusion criteria will not be allowed access to the application.
Require: The Require rule works like an AND logical operator. A user must meet all specified Require rules to be allowed access.

Note: All Access policies must contain an Include rule. This is what defines the initial pool of eligible users who can access an application. You can then add Exclude and Require rules to enforce specific policies for those users.

In addition, You can create addition requirements but using this drop down from email to Ip ranges, to having a valid certificate on the device or Authentication methods like multi factor authentication or sms or One time password to Okta groups as well as Device posture checks like presence of Warp, or Disk encryption or additional device posture checks which I will show you next.
So there are 2 types of device posture checks - one is Client or WARP initiated while the other option is a service provider check that 3rd parties initiate via API
3rd party: So for example with Crowdstrike
Certain Selectors/criteria are checked at login while others are checked continuously. Refer to this doc on the full list.
Identity-based attributes are only checked when a user authenticates, whereas other attributes are polled continuously for changes during the session.
Order of Execution:

Policies are evaluated based on their action type and ordering. Bypass and Service Auth policies are evaluated first, from top to bottom as shown in the UI. Then, Block and Allow policies are evaluated based on their order.
For example, if you have a list of policies arranged as follows:
Allow A
Block B
Service Auth C
Bypass D
Allow E

The policies will execute in this order: Service Auth C > Bypass D > Allow A > Block B > Allow E. Once a user matches an Allow or Block policy, evaluation stops and no subsequent policies can override the decision.

Purpose Justification: when turned on, this presents users with a purpose justification screen directly after they log in to an Access application. This allows organizations to audit not only for who is accessing their resources, but also for why/when they are requesting access. The answers are included in the logs.
Enforce MFA: only available for Okta, Azure AD, OIDC, SAML
Set up an MFA policy through the IdPs available above and then enforce this policy on Cloudflare.
Add a Require action to the rule.
Select Authentication Method and choose mfa - multiple-factor authentication.
Note: The rule must contain an Include rule which defines an identity. For example, the Include rule should allow for users who are part of a user group, email domain, or identity provider group.

Enable the Gateway Proxy. Settings > Network > Enable Proxy for TCP [UDP, ICMP].
Edit the split tunnel entries:

If you are using Exclude mode, the IP ranges you see listed are those that Cloudflare excludes from WARP encryption. If your network’s IP/CIDR range is listed on this page, delete it. For example, the 10.0.0.0/16 that we are routing to below in section “Connect a network”
If you are using Include mode, the IP ranges you see listed are the only ones Cloudflare is encrypting through WARP. Add your network’s IP/CIDR range to the list.
Create a Tunnel:

Typically, App connectors take a while to configure and may have some unknown limitations. CF tunnels make it extremely easy to set up by providing you with a package that you can install using a lightweight daemon called cloudflared. All you have to do is copy and paste the command and point it to a dedicated hostname and this will automatically create a corresponding DNS record if you are using Cloudflare’s DNS and now all you need to do is to add a self-hosted application that will live on the same subdomain or path that you want to map to as dedicated in the previous tunnel config
In addition to routing to different DNS records, you can also route to a Load balancer pool or Kubernetes clusters
Navigate to Access > Tunnels
Create a tunnel.
Enter a name for your tunnel. We suggest choosing a name that reflects the type of resources you want to connect through this tunnel (for example, enterprise-VPC-01).
Select Save tunnel.

Next, you will need to install cloudflared and run it. To do so, check that the environment under Choose an environment reflects the operating system on your machine, then copy the command in the box below and paste it into a terminal window. Run the command.
Once the command has finished running, your connector will appear on the Zero Trust dashboard.
Connect a network:
In the Private Networks tab, add an IP or CIDR (for example 10.0.0.0/16).
Select Save <tunnel-name>.
Continue to create ZT Policies by navigating to Access > Applications > Add an application -> Private Network
Name your application.
For Application type, select Destination IP.
For Value, enter the IP address for your application (for example, 10.128.0.7).
If you would like to create a policy for an IP/CIDR range instead of a specific IP address, you can build a Gateway Network policy using the Destination IP selector.
Configure your App Launcher visibility and logo.
Select Next. You will see two auto-generated Gateway Network policies: one that allows access to the destination IP and another that blocks access.
Modify the policies to include additional identity-based conditions. For example:
Policy 1
Action	Selector	Operator	Value
Allow	Destination IP	in	10.128.0.7
User email	Matches regex	.*@example.com
Policy 2
Action	Selector	Operator	Value
Block	Destination IP	in	10.128.0.7
Policies are evaluated in numerical order, so a user with an email ending in @example.com will be able to access 10.128.0.7 while all others will be blocked
Select Add application
End users can now reach HTTP or TCP-based services on your network by navigating to any IP address in the range you have specified.
Troubleshooting:
Check your Split Tunnel Config: Make sure the IPs/range you want to route to are appropriately deleted in an exclude mode or included in the include mode.
Device configuration:
To check that their device is properly configured, the user can visit https://help.teams.cloudflare.com/ to ensure that:
The page returns Your network is fully protected.
In HTTP filtering, both WARP and Gateway Proxy are enabled.
The Team name matches the Zero Trust organization from which you created the tunnel.
​​Router configuration:

Check the local IP address of the device and ensure that it does not fall within the IP/CIDR range of your private network. For example, some home routers will make DHCP assignments in the 10.0.0.0/24 range, which overlaps with the 10.0.0.0/8 range used by most corporate private networks. When a user’s home network shares the same IP addresses as the routes in your tunnel, their device will be unable to connect to your application.
To resolve the IP conflict, you can either:
Reconfigure the user’s router to use a non-overlapping IP range. Compatible routers typically use 192.168.1.0/24, 192.168.0.0/24 or 172.16.0.0/24.
Tighten the IP range in your tunnel configuration to exclude the 10.0.0.0/24 range. This will only work if your private network does not have any hosts within 10.0.0.0/24

Opening Statement
Gateway is a core aspect of our platform and it’ll be help protect all of your users or office networks from malware, phishing, over any port and protocol.
Key Features
Cloudflare's Gateway and Zero-trust solutions provide several benefits for businesses and organizations, including:

Enhanced security: Cloudflare's Zero-trust solution helps organizations establish secure connections and access controls to their networks and applications, ensuring that only authorized users and devices can access sensitive information. Gateway, a vital component of CF ZT, provides protection against malware, phishing, and other online threats by blocking malicious traffic on the public internet and before it reaches the organization's network.

Improved performance: By leveraging Cloudflare's global network of data centers, Gateway and Zero-trust solutions can reduce latency and improve network performance. This is particularly important for organizations with distributed teams and remote workers who need to access applications and data from different locations. Like the rest of our services, Gateway runs in every single PoP that CF offers so your employees will onramp at the closest CF Datacenter where all of the policies will be applied

Simplified management: Cloudflare's solutions are designed to be easy to set up and manage, with a user-friendly interface and centralized dashboard that provides real-time visibility into network activity and security events. This makes it easier for IT teams to monitor and manage their network and security policies from a single location.

Cost savings: Cloudflare's solutions can help organizations save money by reducing the need for on-premise hardware and software, as well as the costs associated with maintaining and upgrading legacy systems. Additionally, Cloudflare's pay-as-you-go pricing model means that organizations only pay for the services they use, without the need for long-term contracts or upfront fees.

Overall, Cloudflare's Gateway and Zero-trust solutions provide a comprehensive and cost-effective way for organizations to secure their networks and protect their sensitive information from online threats.

Action: Configure DLP Profiles [Data-in-Transit] ENT Only

DLP complements Secure Web Gateway to detect sensitive data transferred in HTTP requests. DLP scans the entire HTTP body, which may include uploaded or downloaded files, chat messages, forms, and other web content. Visibility varies depending on the site or application.
Note: DLP does not scan non-HTTP traffic such as email, nor does it scan any traffic that bypasses Cloudflare Gateway (for example, traffic that matches a Do Not Inspect rule).
Supported file types:
Txt, csv, Microsoft Office 2007 and later (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx), PDF, ZIP files containing the above.
Size: Max file size is 100mb, Size limitation is assessed against the file after unzipping
Predefined Profile: CF currently has 2 prebuilt profiles: Financial info and National Identifiers

Credit card numbers begin with a six or eight-digit Issuer Identification Number (IIN) and are followed by up to 23 additional digits. Credit card numbers must adhere to Luhn’s algorithm as a method of validation.
ABA routing numbers and IBAN are validated algorithmically with check digits.
Click on Financial Information -> Configure -> Turn Status on for the entries you’d like to detect
Advanced Settings:

Match count greater than: refers to the number of times that any enabled entry in the profile can be detected before an action is triggered, such as blocking or logging. For example, if you select a match count of 10, the scanned file or HTTP body must contain 11 or more matching strings. Detections do not have to be unique.
Custom profile:
Anything that doesn’t fit out of the box with our predefined profiles, you can add a custom profile for us to detect.
Select Add custom entry and give it a name.
In Value, enter a regular expression (or regex) that defines the text pattern you want to detect. For example, “test\d\d” will detect the word ‘test’ followed by 2 digits.
Regexes are written in Rust. We recommend validating your regex with Rustexp.
Detected text patterns are limited to 1024 bytes in length.
Regexes with ‘+’ are not supported as they are prone to exceeding the length limit. For example ‘a+’ can detect an infinite number of a’s. We recommend using ‘a{min,max}’ instead, such as ‘a{1,1024}’
To save the detection entry, select Done.

Action: Proxy Endpoints: Source
If you’re unable to deploy the WARP agent but would still like to apply Gateway HTTP policies at the browser level, you can configure a PAC (Proxy Auto-Configuration) file.

The PAC file contains a Javascript function which instructs a browser to forward traffic to a proxy server instead of directly to the destination server. When end users visit a website, their browser will send the request to a Cloudflare proxy server associated with your account, to be filtered by Gateway.
Background: A PAC file is a text file that specifies which traffic should redirect to the proxy server.
Pre-reqs:
Install the Cloudflare root certificate on your device
Installation:
1. Generate a proxy endpoint [via Dash]
Navigate to Gateway > Proxy Endpoints.
Click Create endpoint.
Give your endpoint any name.
Enter the public source IP address of your device(s) in CIDR notation. For example,
Click Save endpoint and confirm the endpoint creation.
Your Cloudflare proxy server domain is of the form:
https://<SUBDOMAIN>.proxy.cloudflare-gateway.com
2. Generate via API
First Test:
In Zero Trust, create an HTTP policy for testing purposes. For example:
Selector	Operator	Value	Action
Domain	in	example.com	Block
Verify that nothing is returned by a curl command:
curl -4 -p -x https://3ele0ss56t.proxy.cloudflare-gateway.com https://example.com

If curl returns a 401 code, it means the public IP of your device does not match the one used to generate the proxy server. Make sure that WARP is turned off on your device and double-check that curl is not using IPv6 (use the -4 option to force IPv4).
Create a PAC file:
Below is the default PAC file. You can customize the file and host it somewhere your browser can access, such as on an internal web server or on Cloudflare Workers.
function FindProxyForURL(url, host) {
// No proxy for private (RFC 1918) IP addresses (intranet sites)
if (
isInNet(dnsResolve(host), "10.0.0.0", "255.0.0.0") ||
return "DIRECT";
// No proxy for localhost
if (isInNet(dnsResolve(host), "127.0.0.0", "255.0.0.0")) {
return "DIRECT";
// Proxy all
return "HTTPS 3ele0ss56t.proxy.cloudflare-gateway.com:443";
Important Notes:
Make sure the directive used for the endpoint is HTTPS and not PROXY.
You must use a PAC file instead of configuring the endpoint directly in the proxy configuration of the browser. This is because modern browsers still do not support HTTPS proxies without PAC files.
Use a proper text editor such as VSCode to avoid added characters.
Configure Browser: Firefox

All major browsers support PAC files. You can configure individual browsers, or you can configure system settings that apply to all browsers on the device. Multiple devices can call the same PAC file as long as their source IP addresses were included in the proxy endpoint configuration.
1. In Firefox, go to Settings and scroll down to Network Settings.
2. Select Settings.
3. Select Automatic proxy configuration URL.
4. Enter the URL where your PAC file is hosted, for example https://proxy-pac.cflr.workers.dev/3ele0ss56t.pac.
5. Select OK. HTTP traffic from Firefox is now being filtered by Gateway.​​
Test: Test your HTTP policy
you can test any supported HTTP policy, such as the example policy created in Step 2. When you go to https://example.com in your browser, you should see the Gateway block page.
When a browser is connected to Gateway by a proxy endpoint, the browser does not perform DNS lookups. Only HTTP policies will apply.
Limitations: At this time, the agentless HTTP proxy does not support identity-based policies or mTLS authentication.

CASB [Demo Vignette 4] Data-at-rest [In-Line DLP]
Opening Statement

Data Loss Prevention complements Cloudflare CASB to detect sensitive data stored in your SaaS applications. Unlike data-in-transit scans which read files sent through Cloudflare Gateway, CASB retrieves files directly via API. Therefore, Gateway and WARP settings (such as Do Not Inspect and Split Tunnel rules) will not affect data-at-rest scans.

Cloudflare’s API-driven Cloud Access Security Broker (CASB) scans SaaS applications for misconfigurations, unauthorized user activity, shadow IT, and other data security issues that can occur after a user has successfully logged in.

When you integrate a third-party SaaS application with Cloudflare CASB, you allow CASB to make API calls to the application and read relevant data on your behalf. The CASB integration permissions are read-only and follow the least privileged model. In other words, only the minimum access required to perform a scan is granted.
Action:
Integration Guide
Add an integration:
1. In Zero Trust, go to CASB > Integrations.
2. Select Add integration.
3. Browse the available SaaS integrations and select the application you would like to add.
4. Follow the step-by-step integration instructions in the UI.
5. To run your first scan, select Save integration. You will be redirected to the Findings page to see an in-depth listing of issues found.

Note: After the first scan, CASB will automatically scan your application on a frequent basis to keep up with any changes. Due to each application having their own set of requirements, scan intervals will vary, but the frequency is typically between every 1 hour and every 24 hours.
Pause an integration: CASB > Integrations > Manage > Turn off Scan findings
Delete an integration: CASB > Integrations > Manage > Delete
Manage security findings:
Prerequisites
You have added a CASB integration.
Your scan has surfaced at least one security finding.
View findings:
1. Navigate to CASB > Findings.
You will see the findings detected across all integrations.
2. To view details for an individual finding, select View.
The individual findings page shows all detected instances of the finding within a specific integration. You can expand an individual row to view details for a particular instance.
3. To resolve the finding, expand the Remediation Guide and follow the step-by-step instructions in the UI.
Other actions you can take include creating an HTTP block policy, updating the finding’s severity level, or hiding irrelevant findings from view.
Severity Levels:
Critical: Suggests the finding is something your team should act on today.
High: Suggests the finding is something your team should act on this week.
Medium: Suggests the finding should be reviewed sometime this month.
Low: Suggests the finding is informational or part of a scheduled review process.
Change the severity level: change the severity level for a finding at any time, in case the default assignment does not suit your environment
1. CASB > Findings.
2. Locate the finding you want to modify and select View.
3. In the severity level drop-down menu, choose your desired setting (Critical, High, Medium, or Low).

The new severity level will only apply to the finding within this specific integration. If you added multiple integrations of the same SaaS application, the other integrations will not be impacted by this change.
Resolve the vulnerability/finding with a Gateway Policy: Prevent/Control access from your findings in minutes.
Pre-Req: Ensure that you have enabled HTTP filtering for your organization.
1. go to CASB > Findings.
2. Locate the finding you want to modify and select View.
3. Find the instance you want to block and select its three-dot menu.
4. Select Block with Gateway HTTP policy. A new browser tab will open with a pre-filled HTTP policy.
Not all CASB findings will have the Block with Gateway HTTP policy option. Unsupported findings can only be resolved from your SaaS application dashboard or through your domain provider.
5. (Optional) Customize the HTTP policy. For example, if the policy blocks an unsanctioned third-party app, you can apply the policy to some or all users, or only block uploads or downloads.
6. Select Save.
Your HTTP policy will now prevent future instances of the security finding.
Hide a finding:
1. go to CASB > Findings.
2. In the Active tab, select the checkboxes for the findings you want to hide.
3. Select Ignore.

The findings will be moved from Active to Ignored. CASB will continue to scan for these findings and report detections in the Ignored tab. You can move ignored findings back to the Active tab at any time.
Hide an instance of a finding
1. Go to CASB > Findings.
2. In the Active tab, locate the finding you want to modify and select View.
3. Under Instances, select the Active tab and locate the instance you want to hide.
4. Select the three-dot menu, then select Hide.

The instance will be moved from Active to Hidden. If the finding occurs again for the same user, CASB will report the new instance in the Hidden tab. You can move hidden instances back to the Active tab at any time.
Scan your SaaS apps with DLP

Combine CASB & DLP to discover if files stored in your SaaS application contain sensitive data. To perform DLP scans in a SaaS app, first configure a DLP profile with the data patterns you want to detect, then enable those profiles in a CASB integration.
Configure a DLP Profile:
Enable DLP Scans in CASB
Add a new integration
Modify an existing integration:
1. Go to CASB > Integrations.
2. Choose a supported integration and select Configure.
3. Under DLP profiles, select the profiles that you want the integration to scan for.
4. Select Save integration.

If you enable a DLP profile from the Manage integrations page, CASB will only scan publicly accessible files that have had a modification event since enabling the DLP profile. Modification events include changes to the following attributes:
Contents of the file
Name of the file
Visibility of the file (only if changed to publicly accessible)
Owner of the file
Location of the file (for example, moved to a different folder)
In order to scan historical data, you must enable the DLP profile during the integration setup flow.
Currently supported Integrations: Google Workspace
Limitations: DLP will only scan:
Files that are visible to anyone on the Internet.
Text-based files such as documents, spreadsheets, and PDFs. Images are not supported.
Files ≤ 100 MB
